ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Indo-Chinese crispy fried fish specifically. FOODON_03302051 (fried fish food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, battered or breaded, fried) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Indo-Chinese style crispy fried fish with aromatics and soy sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific prepared fish dish.

main_ingredients: fish (whole or fillet, likely basa, tilapia, or similar white fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of Dragon Fish is approximately 300 g, representing a shared starter or individual main course portion as typically served at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, fried in batter / Indo-Chinese fried fish — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (fried fish, battered)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Dragon Fish' exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for fried fish (battered) with USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried fish and stir-fried aromatics (ginger, garlic, onion, capsicum, cashew, soy sauce). The dish is a deep-fried fish tossed with Indo-Chinese aromatics. Macros reflect a composite estimate: deep-fried battered fish (~60% of dish weight) plus stir-fried aromatics and cashew (~40%). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried fish and mixed fish dishes — analogous estimate based on battered fried fish and mixed vegetable stir-fry components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dragon Fish (Indo-Chinese crispy fried fish). GI is estimated at 48 (low) based on analogous entries from the University of Sydney GI Database: battered fried fish has a low GI (~45–52) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption; the addition of cashews (GI ~22), capsicum, onion, and ginger does not raise the overall GI materially. The batter coating contributes moderate carbohydrate but the overall dish GI is estimated at 48 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (48 × 35.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.0, rounded to 16.9. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (~420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Dragon Fish due to its high sodium load from soy sauce; a 300 g restaurant serving may provide approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low GI (estimated 48); deep-frying adds significant fat and calories
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dragon Fish has a low estimated glycemic index (~48) which is favourable for blood glucose management, but the high caloric density from deep-frying (~218 kcal/100 g) and a 300 g restaurant portion (~654 kcal) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in moderation and account for the total energy and fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; soy sauce contributes high sodium; cashews provide beneficial unsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation method significantly increases total fat content (~13.2 g/100 g) and the high sodium from soy sauce (~420 mg/100 g) are concerns for cardiovascular health; however, the fish provides omega-3 fatty acids and cashews contribute heart-healthy monounsaturated fats, so occasional moderate consumption within a balanced diet is acceptable for most cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Fish is an Indo-Chinese fusion dish served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, most notably at establishments such as Mr Tandoor. The dish consists of a whole fish or fish fillets (typically a white-fleshed fish such as basa, tilapia, or similar) that are coated in a seasoned batter and deep-fried until crispy and golden. The fried fish is then tossed in a wok with finely chopped ginger, garlic, onions, capsicum (bell pepper), cashew nuts, and soy sauce, producing a dish that combines the crispy texture of deep-fried fish with the bold, savoury, and slightly sweet flavours of Indo-Chinese cooking. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior, tender fish interior, and a fragrant, umami-rich sauce coating. Nutritionally, Dragon Fish is calorie-dense due to deep-frying (~218 kcal per 100 g), with moderate protein (~14.5 g/100 g), significant fat (~13.2 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (~11.8 g/100 g) from the batter and vegetables. The sodium content is high (~420 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index is low (~48), which is favourable for blood glucose management, though the high caloric density warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains fish, soy, gluten (batter), and tree nut (cashew) allergens. It is typically served as a shared starter or individual main course in Indian-Chinese restaurant settings in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is Dragon Fish at Mr Tandoor?
Is Dragon Fish good for diabetics?
What fish is used in Dragon Fish?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Dragon Fish exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo). Nutrient values are estimated from composite analysis of deep-fried battered fish and Indo-Chinese aromatics using ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data for this estimated dish; direct measurement would be required. All other micronutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data. The dish name 'Dragon Fish' is a restaurant menu name, not a species name; the actual fish species used may vary by restaurant. The multilingual names provided are descriptive translations of the dish concept rather than established local-language names, as this is a restaurant-specific menu item. Halal status is assumed based on the Indian Muslim restaurant context (Mr Tandoor, Boat Quay) but has not been independently verified. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on analogous fried fish entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement is recommended. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (48 × 35.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.9.
